scenario: Keystone Bank deploys a new version of its order-process in Camunda 8.8 that adds a user task B between an active service task A and the end event. The platform team must update 3500 instances so each instance continues at service task A and then flows to user task B once service task A completes.
question: How does a migration plan keep service task A active in the target while introducing the new downstream user task B?

options.text: Omit any mapping instruction for element A; the engine auto-detects matching IDs across versions and continues at A by name, then routes to user task B after completion
Provide a mapping instruction from source element A to target element B; the engine moves the active token to user task B and skips service task A in the target definition
Provide a mapping instruction from source element ID A to target element ID A; the engine keeps the active instance at service task A and reaches user task B once A completes
Provide a runtime instruction terminating the instance after service task A; the engine recreates the instance on the target so it begins at user task B from the start event

a.text: Incorrect. Migration requires an explicit migration plan with mapping instructions; element IDs are not auto-detected across versions by name without those instructions being supplied.

b.text: Incorrect. Mapping A to B would move the active token to user task B and skip the still-needed work at service task A on the new definition before reaching B.

c.text: Correct. A mapping instruction from source ID A to target ID A keeps the active instance at service task A, and on completion it flows to the new user task B in the target.

d.text: Incorrect. Runtime instructions terminate an instance after an element; they do not migrate state to a new definition or restart execution at a chosen target element.

explanation: A mapping instruction from source ID A to target ID A keeps the active instance at service task A so it flows to the newly inserted downstream user task B on completion in the target definition.
